english: Mexican Vegetable Soup with Tortilla Strips

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 covers tortilla soup generically; no dedicated vegetarian tortilla soup entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soups and broths as a category. No specific vegetarian tortilla soup code exists; closest parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for vegetarian tortilla soup identified in the clinical terminology. Ontology gap acknowledged.

preparation_method: fried tortilla strips added as garnish
allergens: gluten (corn tortilla strips may contain cross-contamination; wheat-based tortillas if used)
dairy (if sour cream or cheese garnish included)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of vegetarian tortilla soup weighing approximately 350 g, served with crispy tortilla strips and optional garnishes such as avocado, sour cream, and shredded cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Soup, tortilla, with vegetables, canned, ready-to-serve — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 06430 and analogous vegetable soup entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for vegetarian tortilla soup as a restaurant dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for tomato-based vegetable soups (NDB 06430, 'Soup, vegetable, canned, condensed'), black bean soup (NDB 06408), and corn tortilla strips (NDB 18364), weighted to reflect a typical restaurant-style vegetarian tortilla soup composition. Energy and macros are consistent with published recipe analyses for this dish category. All values are per 100 g of prepared soup including tortilla strips.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for tomato-based vegetable soups and black beans
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian tortilla soup. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: tomato-based vegetable soup (GI ~38–48), black beans (GI ~30), and corn tortilla (GI ~52). Weighted composite estimate reflects the mixed carbohydrate profile of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 28.7) / 100 ≈ 12.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 8.2 × 3.5 = 28.7 g.

hypertension.guidance: Vegetarian tortilla soup contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,120 mg per standard 350 g bowl), which represents a significant portion of the 1,500–2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request low-sodium broth and limit or omit added cheese and sour cream garnishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from beans and vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 and approximately 7 g of dietary fibre per 350 g serving, vegetarian tortilla soup produces a moderate glycemic load of approximately 12.9 and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and high-fat garnishes such as sour cream and cheese are minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and plant-based protein from black beans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetarian tortilla soup is low in saturated fat (approximately 1.4 g per 350 g serving) and provides plant-based protein and soluble fibre from black beans, which may support LDL cholesterol reduction; however, sodium content should be monitored and high-fat garnishes avoided in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian tortilla soup is a hearty, tomato-based Mexican soup made without meat, featuring a rich broth of blended tomatoes, onion, garlic, jalapeño, cumin, and chilli powder, simmered with black beans, corn kernels, and diced vegetables. The soup is served topped with crispy fried or baked corn tortilla strips, which provide a satisfying crunch and a subtle corn flavour. Optional garnishes include sliced avocado, a dollop of sour cream, shredded cheese, fresh cilantro, and a squeeze of lime juice. The dish originates from Mexican and Southwestern United States culinary traditions and is a fully vegetarian adaptation of the classic tortilla soup, which traditionally includes chicken. Nutritionally, vegetarian tortilla soup is relatively low in calories (approximately 52 kcal per 100 g) and provides a useful source of dietary fibre (approximately 2 g per 100 g) from black beans and vegetables, supporting digestive health and glycaemic control. The black beans contribute plant-based protein and soluble fibre, which may help reduce LDL cholesterol. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion size and garnishes are controlled. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on the broth used, and individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium preparations. In Singapore, this dish is served at Mexican restaurants such as Fiesta Mexico and Three Amigos, typically priced around SGD 12 per bowl.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or other approved primary source entry exists for vegetarian tortilla soup as a restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for analogous components (tomato-based vegetable soup, black bean soup, corn tortilla strips). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of prepared vegetarian tortilla soup including tortilla strips but excluding optional garnishes (avocado, sour cream, cheese). Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite; no exact restaurant-dish entry available. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 for this fully plant-based soup base; confirmed zero as black beans, corn, tomatoes, and vegetable broth contain no vitamin B12 by nature (naturally zero). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in a standard vegetarian tortilla soup preparation (naturally zero). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source entries used for this composite estimate and is not expected to be present in meaningful quantities in this plant-based soup. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source entries used for this composite estimate. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for vegetarian tortilla soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 7. Dietary flags: is_vegan is false because optional garnishes (sour cream, cheese) are commonly served with this dish; the base soup without garnishes may be vegan. is_gluten_free is false because corn tortilla strips may be subject to cross-contamination and some restaurant preparations use wheat-based tortillas. 8. Allergen note: the dish as served at Singapore restaurants may include dairy garnishes; patrons with dairy allergies should confirm preparation details. 9. Sodium content will vary significantly depending on whether commercial or homemade vegetable broth is used; restaurant versions may be higher than the estimated 320 mg per 100 g. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries and should be treated as indicative rather than precise.
